title: Physical Basis for Band Transport and Dimensionality in Amorphous Oxide Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors
authors: Ananth Dodabalapur, Chankeun Yoon, Xiao Wang
abstract: A consistent and widely accepted physical basis for interpretation of charge transport in amorphous oxide semiconductor (AOS) field-effect transistors (FETs), and more generally device physics, has been hampered by uncertainties in crystalline order, dimensionality, and the effects of a significant density of traps. The overarching theme of this paper is to build and justify a much-needed conceptual framework for describing advanced AOS transistors, particularly those with very small channel lengths. Combining new work and selecting prior research results on charge transport and device physics together with literature reports from various groups on morphology, physical properties, electronic structure and percolation effects, the main evidence that is available in support of a trap-influenced band transport picture in quasi-2-dimensional channels in high mobility AOS FETs is presented.
summary: This paper provides a conceptual framework to justify using band-transport models in amorphous oxide semiconductors. By analyzing structural and electronic data, the authors show that despite the amorphous nature, the presence of nanoscale crystalline domains and high carrier mobilities allow for a quasi-2D band transport description. The work reconciles the Multiple Trapping and Release (MTR) model with percolation effects caused by spatial disorder. This is crucial for the accurate design and scaling of advanced AOS-based transistors.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish a consistent physical basis for charge transport and dimensionality in amorphous oxide semiconductor (AOS) FETs, addressing uncertainties regarding the role of crystalline order, high trap density, and the validity of band transport models in nominally amorphous materials.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that high-mobility AOS FETs are best described by a trap-influenced band transport model in a quasi-2D channel, supported by evidence of nanoscale crystalline domains and delocalized carrier transport.
method: The study combines structural analysis (FEM, TEM, STEM) with electronic transport measurements (Hall effect, carrier velocity) and theoretical modeling (Schrödinger-Poisson analysis, MTR and percolation models).
model_or_system: The system consists of Amorphous Oxide Semiconductor (AOS) FETs, such as IGZO and ZTO. The transport is modeled using an extended Multiple Trapping and Release (MTR) framework combined with percolation theory to account for spatial inhomogeneities.
key_observables: Carrier mobility, carrier density, Hall voltage, carrier velocity, mean free path, and subband energies.
important_parameters: Effective mass (m*), De Broglie wavelength, nanocrystalline domain size (1-5 nm), mean free path (> 1 nm), and the metal-insulator transition threshold (e^2/h).
main_assumptions: Use of effective medium approximation, parabolic band assumption, and the assumption that the accumulation layer can be treated as a quasi-2D system.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows crystalline domain sizes and the relationship between De Broglie wavelength and carrier density; Figure 2 displays the Density of States and Fermi level positions; Figure 3 plots the temperature dependence of various scattering mechanisms and mobility; Figure 4 illustrates the impact of spatial non-uniformity and velocity saturation.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of transport interpretation in AOS, moves to structural evidence of nanocrystalline domains, presents electronic evidence for band transport and 2D confinement, develops a mathematical framework for MTR and percolation, and concludes by integrating these models to describe high-field transport.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Measured Hall voltage as a function of DC magnetic field for a ZTO TFT at room temperature. Carrier density is determined by 𝒏= 𝒓𝑯𝑰𝒅𝑩(𝒒𝒅𝑽) ⁄ . (b) De Broglie wavelength and Classical turning point distance as functions of total induced sheet carrier density, showing that for most carrier densities, the classical turning point is less than the de Broglie wavelength. (c) Illustration of the length scales of interest, including the ranges of effective quantum well thickness, De Broglie wavelength, and mean free path as a function of carrier density. The range of reported crystalline domain size of various oxide semiconductors is also shown for comparison. For calculations in (b)...

Figure 2. (a) Calculated density of states (DOS) as a function of energy. The band-state DOS is calculated as 1.42×1014 cm-2eV-1 for E > 0 eV, while for E < 0 eV, the trap DOS is 6.5×1013× 𝒆𝑬/𝟎.𝟎𝟗𝟗𝟏𝒆𝑽 cm-2eV-1 for E < 0 eV. The dashed lines indicate Fermi levels, EF, corresponding to carrier densities of n2D = 1012 and 1013 cm-2. (b) Calculated mean free path as a function of carrier density in the band at different temperatures. The colored horizontal dashed lines are the mean free path values in the non-degenerate limit based on Eq. 9. The colored solid lines are for the mean free path in more general conditions based on Eq. 10 and 11. A value 𝝁𝟎= 𝟐𝟎 𝐜𝐦𝟐/𝐕𝐬 for the nominal band mobility...

Fig. 3. Temperature dependence of (a) 𝝁𝑻𝑪, (b) 𝝁𝑶𝑷, (c) 𝝁𝒂𝒗𝒈−𝒃𝒂𝒏𝒅, and (d) 𝝁𝒕𝒉𝒊𝒏−𝒇𝒊𝒍𝒎 for different sheet carrier densities. Calculations were conducted with Cox = 0.75 μF/cm2, Vov = 1 V ~ 4 V, using NT = 6.5×1012/cm2, Tta = 1150 K, εs = 10, ε∞ = 4, and ħ =30 meV
Fig. 4 Schematic illustration of percolation conduction in disordered amorphous semiconductors: (a) real-space percolation pathways and (b) the corresponding energy landscape.

Fig. 6. (a) Calculated density of states (DOS) as a function of energy. The dashed lines indicate the band edge and the Fermi level at the metal-insulator transition criterion. The critical band carrier density is 1.2×1013/cm2, corresponding to EF = 84meV. (b) Schematic classification of transport regime in terms of conductivity and mean free path, showing hopping, delocalized band transport, and metallic regimes.

Fig. 7 (a) 2D conductivity of the ZTO TFT from Ref. 22. The minimum 2D metallic conductivity is illustrated by the purple dashed line. (b) Measured mobility of this ZTO TFT as a function of gate voltage. Von is the on-voltage of the transistor 76 (analogous to the threshold voltage). For reference, the sheet carrier density, which increases linearly with Vg – Von, is ~1.15×1013 cm-2 for (Vg – Von ) = 7.7 V.
